---
title: "qgtools: End-to-End Tutorial (Backends, HPC, and External Libraries)"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
execute:
  eval: false
---

## Introduction

This tutorial demonstrates a complete workflow for computing genetic risk scores (GRS) using `qgtools`.

It covers:

- Internal backends (C++ and Fortran)
- External high-performance backend (`libqgtools`)
- OpenMP-based parallel execution
- Benchmarking and validation
- HPC-ready setup

The goal is to show how a **single R interface** can scale seamlessly from a portable implementation to high-performance execution.

---

## Setup

### Environment (Linux / HPC)

```bash
conda install -n base -c conda-forge mamba

mamba create -n rcsg   -c conda-forge   r-base r-devtools r-remotes r-xml r-rcurl r-pkgbuild   zlib compilers make pkg-config "libblas=*=*mkl"

conda activate rcsg
```

### HPC session

```bash
srun --pty -c 4 --mem=10g --time=20:00:00 --job-name=rcsg --account=csgtools bash
```

### Thread configuration

```bash
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4
export OMP_PROC_BIND=spread
export OMP_PLACES=cores
export OMP_DYNAMIC=FALSE

export OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS=1
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=1
```

### Install and load R packages

```r
library(devtools)
pak::pak("psoerensen/qgtools")

library(qgtools)
library(qgg)
library(data.table)
library(bench)
```

---

## Data and Inputs

```r
setwd("/faststorage/project/csgtools/data/plink")

files <- c("bed", "bim", "fam", "pheno", "covar")

for (f in files) {
  url <- sprintf(
    "https://github.com/psoerensen/qgdata/raw/main/simulated_human_data/human.%s",
    f
  )
  download.file(url, destfile = paste0("human.", f), mode = "wb")
}

Glist <- gprep(
  study = "Example",
  bedfiles = "human.bed",
  bimfiles = "human.bim",
  famfiles = "human.fam"
)

chr <- 1
file <- Glist$bedfiles[chr]
n <- Glist$n
m <- Glist$mchr[chr]
nt <- 50

cls <- sample.int(m)
af  <- runif(m, 0.05, 0.5)

S <- matrix(rnorm(m * nt), nrow = m, ncol = nt)
```

---

## Running the Model

```r
invisible(qgtools:::mtgrsbed(file, n, cls, af, S,
                             nthreads = 12,
                             backend = "internal_cpp"))

invisible(qgtools:::mtgrsbed(file, n, cls, af, S,
                             nthreads = 12,
                             backend = "internal_fortran"))

res <- bench::mark(
  cpp = qgtools:::mtgrsbed(file, n, cls, af, S,
                           backend = "internal_cpp"),
  fortran = qgtools:::mtgrsbed(file, n, cls, af, S,
                               backend = "internal_fortran"),
  iterations = 5,
  check = FALSE
)

print(res)
```

---

## Validation

```r
grs_cpp <- qgtools:::mtgrsbed(file, n, cls, af, S,
                              backend = "internal_cpp")

grs_fortran <- qgtools:::mtgrsbed(file, n, cls, af, S,
                                  backend = "internal_fortran")

tol <- 1e-10
stopifnot(isTRUE(all.equal(grs_cpp, grs_fortran, tolerance = tol)))
```

---

## External Backend

```r
qgtools:::install_libqgtools()

grs_external <- qgtools:::mtgrsbed(
  file, n, cls, af, S,
  backend = "external_cpp"
)

stopifnot(isTRUE(all.equal(grs_cpp, grs_external, tolerance = tol)))
```

---

## Architecture

```
User (R)
  ↓
mtgrsbed()
  ↓
Backend selection
  ↓
----------------------------------------------
| internal_cpp      (C++ implementation)     |
| internal_fortran  (Fortran implementation) |
| external_cpp      (shared library plugin)  |
----------------------------------------------
```

---

## Takeaways

- Single R interface, multiple implementations
- Portable → high-performance transition
- Numerical consistency ensured
- Modular and extensible design
